## [12:07 - 15:10]

You've taught them how to sell a product. And so what we want to do is we want to establish that baseline first. So there's a module of content on the platform called the Fundamentals of Selling. So after you teach them how to sell, you give them your introductory sales course, then you start teaching them how to sell your product, not product before sales. So that's one thing that you definitely want to understand. And let's talk about some other processes that don't really work. So the sink or swim thing, which we talked about. You know, here's the job, here's what you've got to do, a little smack on the tail and get to work. So we know that that really doesn't work. Occasionally, you'll find a diamond in the rough, but what's going to happen more often than not, people are going to stay with you for two, three, four months, maybe, and then they're going to blow out. And the problem with that is Career Builder did a study recently and it showed that the average employee that stays less than six months, if they're making less than $70,000 a year, will cost you at minimum $30,000. I was talking to a dealer the other day, he said, at minimum it would cost you 30. It could cost you as much as 50 or 60. So hiring the wrong people, keeping the wrong people around, not getting people on board in the right way, not training them right in the beginning. We know that people have really short attention spans.

## [15:10 - 18:11]

They can't stay focused for three or four or five hours, let alone probably 20 or 30 or 40 minutes on one thing before they just start checking out, right? Start yawning, getting tired, getting distracted, looking at all this over here, wondering, man, is this what this is going to be like? I want it to be excited, right? So we know that that doesn't work either. So now what can you do that will work, right? So we know that the sink or swim doesn't work. We know that sitting in a room and doing lectures all day, that ain't going to work either. So what can we do that works? Well we know that the blended approach works best, where you deliver some content and then you have the practical attached behind it. So deliver content, practical. We actually have a process that we use for training and developing and transferring skill that we found to be really effective. Number one is content delivery, consistent content delivery. Two is discussion about the material to kind of iron out all the wrinkles. Three is the practical, the role play, actually doing what we've taught or what we've communicated. And then the fourth time is just the repetition, the duration of time that you spend on it, going over it, over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over and over again and again, right? It's all about building that muscle in place to where people know it so well they can't forget it. So no sink or swim, no all day lecture, we have to have some type of blended approach where we're incorporating some lecture, we're incorporating some discussion, we're incorporating some role play and then we're building on that incrementally. So technically it's not a cold call, but it's pretty close. And so we put people down really early, probably second day and have them make cold calls. And it's brutal. So what I would recommend that when you're bringing people new on board, you need to push them into situations that are going to make them uncomfortable very fast. Why? Because that's when you're going to find out who they really are. ## [18:11 - 21:12]

You're there to support. What we do is we incrementally build on the success that we've had before and we don't lose the skill that we've just visited. This happens a lot in continuous education and dealerships. What happens is you have one or two sales meetings a week that are more of a meeting than they are of a training, but we call them training when they really aren't.
